food_name: Vietnamese Nem Nuong (Grilled Pork Sausage Platter)
food_subgroup: Grilled meat platter with accompaniments (vermicelli, fresh vegetables, herbs, dipping sauce)

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Vietnamese nem nuong. FOODON_03311737 (pork sausage food product) is used as the closest parent. The full dish includes vermicelli, fresh vegetables, herbs, and dipping sauce not captured by this term.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FMM (Pork meat products) is the closest defensible parent. No specific code exists for nem nuong as a composite Vietnamese dish with accompaniments.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for Vietnamese nem nuong or sufficiently close composite dish. Assigned none.

serving.description: 2 pieces nem nuong with vermicelli, vegetables, herbs, and dipping sauce
serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving of Vietnamese nem nuong consists of 2 grilled pork sausage pieces (approximately 80–100 g of sausage) accompanied by rice vermicelli, fresh lettuce, herbs, and a homemade dipping sauce, totalling approximately 280 g.

nutrient_source.title: Nem nuong / Vietnamese grilled pork sausage — composite estimate derived from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries: Ground Pork (NDB 10006), Rice Vermicelli cooked (NDB 20050), Fresh Vegetables and Herbs (NDB 11233, 11165), and Fish Sauce (NDB 12174); weighted by typical recipe proportions
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central SR Legacy
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct entry for Vietnamese nem nuong exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy component ingredients weighted by standard recipe proportions: approximately 36% ground pork sausage, 30% cooked rice vermicelli, 20% fresh vegetables and herbs, 8% dipping sauce, 6% rice paper. Energy and macros are calculated from these weighted components.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; rice vermicelli GI = 53 (boiled, white); composite dish GI estimated from dominant carbohydrate source
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Vietnamese nem nuong as a composite dish. The dominant carbohydrate source is rice vermicelli (cooked white rice vermicelli, GI = 53, University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care). The pork sausage component contributes negligible glycemic load. GI of 53 is assigned as an analogous estimate based on the rice vermicelli component. GL per serving calculated as (GI × available carbohydrate per serving in grams) / 100 = (53 × 40.6) / 100 ≈ 21.5, where available carbohydrate per 280 g serving = 14.5 × 2.8 = 40.6 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from fish sauce and seasoning (~420 mg per 100 g; ~1176 mg per serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit nem nuong consumption due to its high sodium load from fish sauce and seasoning; a single serving may contribute approximately 50% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg, and portion control or requesting reduced-sauce versions is strongly advised.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load (GL ~21.5 per serving) driven by rice vermicelli; moderate carbohydrate content of 14.5 g per 100 g
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should be mindful of the rice vermicelli component which drives a moderate glycemic load of approximately 21.5 per serving; pairing with additional non-starchy vegetables and limiting the vermicelli portion can help moderate postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat (~2.7 g per 100 g) and high sodium from pork fat and fish sauce
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For individuals with cardiovascular disease, nem nuong presents a moderate concern due to its saturated fat content from ground pork and pork fat, combined with high sodium from fish sauce; limiting serving frequency and choosing leaner pork preparations where available is recommended.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Nem Nướng (Vietnamese Grilled Pork Sausage) is a traditional dish originating from Central Vietnam, particularly associated with the city of Nha Trang. It consists of seasoned ground pork mixed with pork fat, garlic, fish sauce, sugar, and a leavening agent such as baking powder, shaped into cylindrical sausages or patties and grilled over charcoal until caramelised and slightly charred on the outside while remaining juicy inside. The dish is typically served as a platter with rice vermicelli (bún), fresh lettuce leaves, Vietnamese herbs such as mint and perilla, sliced cucumber, bean sprouts, and rice paper for wrapping, accompanied by a homemade dipping sauce that is often peanut-based or a sweet-savoury nước chấm variant. The flavour profile is smoky, savoury, and slightly sweet with a tender, springy texture from the pork. Nutritionally, nem nuong is a moderate-energy mixed dish providing approximately 168 kcal per 100 g, with meaningful protein (~10 g) from pork and moderate carbohydrate (~14.5 g) from vermicelli. Sodium content is elevated (~420 mg per 100 g) due to fish sauce, making it a concern for hypertension management. The glycemic index is estimated at 53 (low) based on the rice vermicelli component, though the glycemic load per full serving is moderate at approximately 21.5. In Singapore, nem nuong is served at Vietnamese restaurants and is popular as a sharing platter or light meal. The dish is not halal-certified as it contains pork.
common_user_queries: How many calories in Vietnamese grilled pork sausage?
Is nem nuong high in sodium?
What is the GI of nem nuong?
What does nem nuong come with?
What is the difference between nem nuong and cha gio?

confidence_reason: No direct entry for Vietnamese nem nuong exists in any primary regional approved source (HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, FAO/INFOODS SEA). Nutrient values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy component ingredients weighted by standard recipe proportions. GI is estimated from the dominant carbohydrate source (rice vermicelli, GI=53) per University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium due to the composite estimation approach.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates derived from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy weighted by typical recipe proportions; no direct analytical data for nem nuong as a complete dish was found in approved primary sources. 2. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy component entries used for this estimation. 3. Vitamin E is -1 as it is not reliably reported across all component entries and the composite estimate would carry high uncertainty. 4. Selenium is -1 as it is not consistently reported in the component entries used. 5. GI of 53 is an analogous estimate based on rice vermicelli as the dominant carbohydrate source per University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2008. 6. The dish as served at Co Chung Restaurant (Singapore) is not halal-certified as it contains pork. 7. The food_name has been normalised from the promotional field survey title 'Vietnamese Nem Nuong 2PCS - MUST TRY' to the canonical English name 'Vietnamese Nem Nuong (Grilled Pork Sausage Platter)'. 8. Serving size of 280 g is estimated for a 2-piece nem nuong platter with standard accompaniments. 9. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, potassium, calcium, iron, cholesterol, vitamin C, vitamin A, B vitamins, folate, phosphorus, magnesium, and zinc are estimated from USDA SR Legacy component data and carry moderate uncertainty. 10. This dish is Vietnamese in origin and cuisine; the cuisine field reflects Vietnamese origin rather than the field survey listing cuisine tag of Singapore.
